steps.title: Find the slope and properties of the given line
Test Equation I: $6x + 7.5y = 15$
steps.content: Check ratios: $\frac{6}{4} = 1.5$, $\frac{7.5}{5} = 1.5$, $\frac{15}{10} = 1.5$. All ratios equal, so Equation I is $1.5 \times$ the original $\Rightarrow$ same line $\Rightarrow$ infinitely many solutions. At least one solution exists.

steps.title: Test Equation II: $6x - 7.5y = 15$
steps.content: Check slope: $-7.5y = -6x + 15 \Rightarrow y = \frac{4}{5}x - 2$. Slope $= \frac{4}{5} \neq -\frac{4}{5}$ (different sign). Different slopes $\Rightarrow$ lines intersect at exactly one point $\Rightarrow$ one solution. At least one solution exists.

steps.title: Evaluate all four answer choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — I only: Equation I gives infinitely many solutions ✓, but Equation II also gives at least one solution ✓, so “I only” is too restrictive. ✗
  • Choice B — II only: Equation II gives exactly one solution ✓, but Equation I also gives at least one solution ✓, so “II only” is too restrictive. ✗
  • Choice C — I and II: Both Equation I (infinitely many solutions) and Equation II (exactly one solution) satisfy the condition of having at least one solution. ✓
  • Choice D — Neither I nor II: Both equations do yield at least one solution, so “Neither” is incorrect. ✗

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Both equations can serve as the second equation in a system with at least one solution. C is correct.
